title: Chapter 0: How To Use This Architecture Guide
content:
# Tutor System Architecture

This guide explains how Tutor works as a system. It is written for a reader who understands ordinary software ideas but should not need to know this codebase already.

Read each chapter with four questions:

1. What responsibility does this part own?
2. What information enters and leaves it?
3. What record proves that the work happened?
4. What is implemented now, and what remains a target?

Status labels have precise meanings:

- **Completed locally**: implemented in the current local application and covered by source or tests.
- **Partly completed**: the main path exists, but an important proof, provider, or quality gate remains.
- **Planned**: accepted direction that is not yet enforced by the runtime.
- **Deferred**: intentionally outside the local-beta phase.

Tutor is an application built around existing language, speech, and retrieval models. It does not retrain a foundation model after every lesson. The learner brain is the product's learning record. [Graphify](https://github.com/rajinwonderland/graphify) is a separate repository map used by developers.

title: Chapter 1: The Product And Its Learning Loop
content:
# The Product And Its Learning Loop

Tutor connects reading, conversation, memory, practice, and inspection into one learning loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Source[PDF or open study] --> Teach[Chat and voice teaching]
  Teach --> Evidence[Learning evidence]
  Evidence --> Brain[Learner brain]
  Brain --> Adapt[Adapt the next explanation]
  Brain --> Revision[Build revision material]
  Brain --> Admin[Inspect and correct]
```

| Surface | What it is responsible for |
| --- | --- |
| Study | Opens PDFs, captures selections, and provides document context. |
| Chat and voice | Explain, ask questions, use bounded tools, and respond in real time. |
| Learner brain | Stores books, concepts, evidence, mastery estimates, artifacts, and corrections. |
| Revision | Turns saved learning into readable chapters and active-recall practice. |
| Analytics | Summarizes recorded learning patterns without changing them. |
| Admin | Lets an operator inspect a learner, a request, or a readiness problem. |

The important boundary is between **conversation** and **evidence**. A fluent answer can help immediately, but it does not prove that the learner understands the idea. Durable mastery changes require a validated learner action such as an evaluated answer or a linked flashcard review.

title: Chapter 2: Technology And Service Map
content:
# Technology And Service Map

The stack is easier to understand when each technology is tied to one responsibility.

| Technology or service | Responsibility in Tutor |
| --- | --- |
| React and TypeScript | Render product surfaces and keep UI contracts typed. |
| Tailwind and GSAP | Style the interface and animate meaningful state changes. |
| Zustand | Hold short-lived state shared between visible screens. |
| Dexie and IndexedDB | Persist the local learner brain in the browser. See the [Dexie documentation](https://dexie.org/docs). |
| Express, SSE, and WebSockets | Expose server routes and stream chat, voice, tools, and diagnostics. |
| OpenRouter-compatible models | Run foreground teaching and background reasoning behind provider adapters. |
| Deepgram Nova and Aura | Stream speech recognition and speech output. Deepgram recommends one TTS WebSocket per conversation in its [streaming TTS guide](https://developers.deepgram.com/docs/streaming-text-to-speech). |
| react-pdf and Python extraction | Render source pages and extract searchable document text. |
| Mermaid | Render diagrams stored in architecture and learning chapters. |
| Graphify | Map source-code relationships for maintainers; it is not learner data. |

## Current Voice Direction

The target owned voice path keeps a session open between the browser and Tutor's server. Deepgram handles streaming speech-to-text and text-to-speech. A fast foreground model teaches. Background work uses a separate asynchronous lane and returns a compact result to the foreground conversation.

The current runtime also retains fallback paths. They are useful for development, but they must not be described as equivalent to a fully measured duplex production voice system.

title: Chapter 3: Frontend Ownership And State
content:
# Frontend Ownership And State

`src/App.tsx` chooses the active product surface. A view owns a page-level workflow, while a component owns a reusable interaction. Zustand carries only live state that several surfaces need.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Action[User action] --> View[View workflow]
  View --> Component[Focused component]
  Component --> Store[Shared live state]
  Component --> Route[Server route]
  Store --> Render[Updated interface]
  Route --> Render
```

A selected PDF quotation belongs in live state because Chat may need it immediately. A learning book, evidence row, or mastery change belongs in Dexie because it must survive navigation and be auditable later.

## Design Rule

Put data in the narrowest owner that can safely manage it:

- component state for one control;
- view state for one workflow;
- Zustand for live cross-view coordination;
- Dexie for durable learner records;
- server ledgers for provider and request activity.

**Completed locally:** lazy-loaded product surfaces, shared study state, and durable local records. **Planned:** continue reducing broad component responsibilities, especially in large Chat and Admin surfaces.

title: Chapter 4: Study, PDFs, And Source Context
content:
# Study, PDFs, And Source Context

A PDF has two related representations:

1. the rendered page the learner can see;
2. extracted text that retrieval and background work can search.

Neither representation should silently replace the other.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Upload[Upload PDF] --> Render[Render pages]
  Upload --> Extract[Extract text]
  Render --> Select[Selection and annotation]
  Extract --> Retrieve[Context retrieval]
  Select --> Tutor[Foreground tutor]
  Retrieve --> Tutor
  Tutor --> Book[Learning book]
```

The study surface supports several PDFs in one learning book. The active document is given priority, while companion documents can contribute bounded excerpts. Current-page vision is reserved for questions about what is visibly shown; extracted text supports document-level retrieval.

**Completed locally:** PDF upload, local storage, multiple-document switching, rendering, selection, annotations, extraction, and context packet construction. **Partly completed:** document-wide grounding quality and semantic citation verification. **Deferred:** cloud document storage and cross-device sync.

title: Chapter 5: Foreground Teaching And Tool Delegation
content:
# Foreground Teaching And Tool Delegation

The foreground tutor owns the human conversation. It should acknowledge every part of a mixed request, begin teaching immediately when possible, and delegate slower work without losing the conversational thread.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant L as Learner
  participant F as Foreground tutor
  participant B as Background worker
  L->>F: Teach variables and find the current NVIDIA price
  F->>L: I will check that. A variable is...
  F->>B: Search current NVIDIA price
  B-->>F: Source-linked result
  F->>L: Also, the latest price I found is...
```

Bounded tools include current-page inspection, web search, flashcard generation, answer evaluation, and learner-graph proposals. A current fact should use web retrieval; a question about uploaded material should prefer local sources.

Background results must be converted into natural spoken or written language before they are inserted. Raw JSON, Markdown decoration, provider metadata, and internal tool names should never be read aloud.

**Completed locally:** typed tool contracts, request correlation, foreground streaming, and background-result stitching. **Partly completed:** broad real-provider testing across every tool family and consistently smooth insertion during voice interruption.

title: Chapter 6: Local Memory And The Learner Ledger
content:
# Local Memory And The Learner Ledger

During local beta, Dexie is the learner brain's system of record. A ledger is useful because every durable change can be inspected and corrected.

| Record family | What it records |
| --- | --- |
| Learning books, chapters, entries, and concepts | Material that can be studied again. |
| Evidence events and mastery deltas | Learner actions and resulting mastery changes. |
| Artifacts and citation states | Generated material and the scope of its verification. |
| Model, tool, retrieval, memory, and background-job rows | What the runtime attempted. |
| Misconceptions and corrections | Reviewable hypotheses and non-destructive repairs. |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Turn[Completed turn] --> Orchestrator[Memory orchestrator]
  Orchestrator --> Book[Learning material]
  Orchestrator --> Trace[Runtime ledger]
  Evidence[Validated learner action] --> Gate{Evidence contract}
  Gate -->|passes| Delta[Mastery delta]
  Gate -->|fails| Audit[Audit only]
```

A saved model summary is useful context, but it is not proof of knowledge. [Bayesian Knowledge Tracing](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF01099821) estimates changing knowledge from attempts; Tutor therefore keeps model observations separate from validated learner evidence.

**Completed locally:** local books, ledgers, evidence contracts, BKT-backed updates, and non-destructive corrections. **Deferred:** tenant isolation, cloud backups, and synchronized multi-device memory.

title: Chapter 7: Revision Books And Active Recall
content:
# Revision Books And Active Recall

Revision should read like learning material, not a transcript index. A useful chapter needs an explanation, relationships between ideas, an example, a visual model when appropriate, and a check that asks the learner to use the idea.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Conversation[Completed teaching] --> Notes[Structured learning note]
  Notes --> Chapter[Revision chapter]
  Chapter --> Example[Worked example]
  Chapter --> Diagram[Diagram or flowchart]
  Chapter --> Recall[Active-recall check]
  Recall --> Evidence[Validated review evidence]
```

Generated chapters may include Mermaid flowcharts and fenced code blocks when the lesson contains a process or programming example. The content should preserve the actual concept while removing conversational filler such as greetings, repeated prompts, and tool chatter.

A generated flashcard is an artifact. A learner's answer becomes evidence only when it is linked to a concept and evaluated under an explicit contract.

**Completed locally:** built-in books, generated learning books, chapter navigation, diagrams, code rendering, flashcards, and a title-matched stored-audio player. **Partly completed:** consistently rich chapter generation for sparse conversations, stronger source-grounding checks, and regenerated audio guides for rewritten chapter titles.

title: Chapter 8: Admin, Learners, And Interpretation
content:
# Admin, Learners, And Interpretation

Admin should answer three questions quickly:

1. What does this learner appear to know or struggle with?
2. What evidence caused that interpretation?
3. Is the teaching system healthy enough to trust this result?

The learner view groups local learning books by learner name, then renders their concepts and relationships as a knowledge graph. This is a **product graph**. It is separate from Graphify, which maps source code.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Learner[Selected learner] --> Books[Learning books]
  Books --> Concepts[Concept graph]
  Concepts --> Evidence[Evidence and mastery]
  Evidence --> Patterns[Learning patterns]
  Patterns --> Decision[Teaching or review decision]
```

How to read the main signals:

- **Mastery** is an evidence-linked estimate, not a grade.
- **Confidence** describes how strongly the current local records support an interpretation.
- **Active misconceptions** are items requiring review, not proven facts about the learner.
- **Coverage** shows whether chat, voice, context, tools, and storage produced a coherent local proof.

**Completed locally:** per-learner grouping from local books, learner concept inspection, evidence views, activity timelines, and readiness diagnostics. **Partly completed:** real authentication and stable user identifiers. **Deferred:** organization-wide cloud administration.

title: Chapter 9: One Complete Background Workflow
content:
# One Complete Background Workflow

Suppose a learner asks: “Explain Python variables and also find today's NVIDIA price.”

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Ask[Mixed learner request] --> Split{Classify each intention}
  Split --> Teach[Teach variables now]
  Split --> Search[Queue current-price search]
  Search --> Tool[Background web tool]
  Tool --> Normalize[Extract answer, value, time, and sources]
  Normalize --> Stitch[Create a natural bridge sentence]
  Teach --> Speak[Continue foreground response]
  Stitch --> Speak
  Speak --> Save[Save conversation and learning note]
```

A correct implementation does not ignore the second intention and does not pause the lesson until search finishes. It acknowledges the task, teaches, and later inserts a sentence such as “Also, I found the current NVIDIA price...”

The background result must include enough source and timestamp information for the UI to show where the current fact came from. The spoken version should be concise; detailed citations can remain visible in the transcript.

The learning book should save the Python explanation as learning material. The temporary stock price may be stored as a source-linked artifact, but it should not become a durable learner concept unless the lesson was actually about markets.

title: Chapter 10: Models, Voice, And Provider Boundaries
content:
# Models, Voice, And Provider Boundaries

Providers are replaceable adapters. Product behavior should depend on stable contracts rather than one vendor name.

| Job | Current local path | Trust boundary |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Foreground tutoring | OpenRouter-compatible chat model selected in Settings | Generated teaching is a proposal until grounded or validated. |
| Background reasoning and web work | Asynchronous model/tool lane using provider-native or configured tools | Result must return to the foreground with source and request metadata. |
| Streaming speech-to-text | Deepgram live transcription | Transcript is interaction data, not mastery evidence. |
| Streaming text-to-speech | Deepgram Aura WebSocket or explicit fallback | Spoken text must be normalized before synthesis. |
| Current-page vision | Server vision route | Limited to rendered study context. |
| Read aloud | TTS route for completed assistant text | Separate from live duplex conversation. |
| Stored chapter audio | Checked-in audio assets | Avoids live generation during reading. |

Deepgram documents streaming TTS over a persistent WebSocket and supports `Speak`, `Flush`, `Clear`, and `Close` controls in its [TTS WebSocket reference](https://developers.deepgram.com/reference/text-to-speech/speak-streaming). For browser voice systems, persistent sessions reduce repeated handshake cost and make interruption handling possible.

**Completed locally:** provider adapters, Deepgram paths, fallback behavior, request ledgers, and browser-side voice controls. **Partly completed:** measured end-to-end latency and interruption quality under real network conditions. No architecture document should promise a universal sub-200 ms response without measured percentile data.

title: Chapter 11: What Is Completed And What Is Not
content:
# What Is Completed And What Is Not

This chapter is the current implementation audit. It should be updated when a major contract changes.

## Completed Locally

- Study supports local multi-PDF books, rendering, switching, annotations, and extracted context.
- Chat streams foreground responses and exposes bounded document, web, flashcard, evaluation, and graph tools.
- Voice has Deepgram integration paths, interruption controls, transcript handling, and runtime tracing.
- The learner brain stores learning books, concepts, entries, evidence, mastery deltas, artifacts, corrections, and request ledgers in Dexie.
- Mastery increases are gated by validated learner evidence rather than model summaries.
- Revision renders built-in and generated books, Mermaid diagrams, code blocks, and flashcards.
- Revision can play stored audio only when an audio manifest title matches the current chapter title.
- Admin can inspect learners, learner concepts, evidence, system activity, and local-beta readiness.
- Graphify is used as the developer architecture map, with source verification before edits.

## Partly Completed

- Generated learning chapters can be rich, but sparse conversations still need stronger structure and source support.
- Voice and background tools are wired, but every provider combination has not been proven under a repeatable real-key test matrix.
- Citation rows provide provenance and scoped checks; they do not yet prove semantic entailment for every generated claim.
- Per-learner Admin grouping exists locally, but the current identity is a learner name stored with books, not authenticated tenancy.
- The rewritten built-in chapters need regenerated, title-matched MP3 audio guides before audio coverage is complete again.
- Diagnostics can prove local wiring, but production-grade alerts, retention, and operations are not present.

## Not Completed Or Deferred

- AWS deployment as the system of record.
- Secure multi-tenant authentication, authorization, and tenant isolation.
- Cloud backups, cross-device synchronization, and organization administration.
- A universal latency guarantee for voice. Latency must be reported as measured p50, p95, and failure rate for a named route and region.
- Hidden-chain-of-thought access or proof of a model's private reasoning. Runtime traces show app events, not private model internals.

title: Chapter 12: Maintenance, Safety, And Release Gates
content:
# Maintenance, Safety, And Release Gates

The final chapter explains how to change Tutor without confusing an architectural claim with a finished feature.

## High-Risk Boundaries

| Boundary | Failure to watch for |
| --- | --- |
| Dexie schema | Existing learner records become unreadable or incorrectly migrated. |
| Server routes, SSE, and WebSockets | Browser and server disagree about events or payloads. |
| Zustand fields | One view silently breaks another view's live state. |
| Chat stream and tool parser | Partial text, tool results, or citations are inserted incorrectly. |
| Document ingestion | The tutor receives missing or misleading source context. |
| Evidence contracts | Model observations accidentally change mastery. |
| Graphify artifacts | Generated repository maps are edited by hand or refreshed without scope. |

## Required Local Gate

For ordinary source changes, run focused tests, `npm run lint`, and `npm run build`. Behavior changes also require a rendered workflow test. Provider work requires a real-key test record that names model, region, route, timestamp, latency percentiles, and failure count.

## Release Decision

Local beta is ready only when one coherent typed-chat path and one coherent live-voice path can be followed from input, through context and tools, to output and durable records. Synthetic rehearsals are useful for wiring, but they do not replace live provider proof.

Cloud work remains deferred until this local contract is reliable. Useful references include the [OpenAI tools guide](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/tools), [Deepgram streaming TTS guide](https://developers.deepgram.com/docs/streaming-text-to-speech), [Dexie documentation](https://dexie.org/docs), and the original [Bayesian Knowledge Tracing paper](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF01099821).
